title: The Bottle Deposit (2)

jokes.text: If wedding invitations were left up to men, we'd just drive around sticking fliers in windshields.
jokes.setup: Jerry muses about how men would handle wedding invitations
jokes.punchline: They'd treat it like a parking lot flier — drive around sticking them in windshields
jokes.explanation: Solid observational stand-up premise. The image of windshield-flier wedding invitations is vivid and funny. Good economy, clean structure. Not a legendary bit but above average stand-up opener.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You know, when you get a wedding invitation, it's like the most formal thing you can receive. It's on nice cardstock, there's tissue paper inside, calligraphy... But really, what they're saying is 'We're getting married, please come.' That's it. They could just put it on a flyer under your windshield wiper like 'WEDDING - Saturday at 4. Bring a gift.'

jokes.text: Not even typed up either. Just magic markers, xeroxed, you know: 'Party.'
jokes.setup: The windshield flier premise is established
jokes.punchline: Escalates to the absurd minimalism of just writing 'Party.' with a magic marker
jokes.explanation: The escalation to the single word 'Party.' is brilliantly economical. Maximum comedy per word. The period after 'Party' does real work — it implies all the effort men would put into wedding communication.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So you just write 'Party' on a piece of paper and stick it on someone's windshield?

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: That's it. That's all you need.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: What if they want more information?

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: They don't. They see 'Party,' they show up.

jokes.text: What's needed is a divorce announcement. 'Mr. and Mrs. Fred Johnson request the honour of your presence at the returning of their daughter back to Mr. and Mrs. Fred Johnson.'
jokes.setup: Jerry has been riffing on wedding invitations — pivots to argue for a different kind of announcement
jokes.punchline: The divorce announcement mimicking formal wedding invitation language, returning the daughter like a product
jokes.explanation: The structural joke of mirroring formal wedding invitation language for a divorce is very clever. 'Returning of their daughter back to Mr. and Mrs. Fred Johnson' is funny because of the transactional framing — she's being returned like a loan. Strong structure and originality for stand-up.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You know, why should wedding announcements be the only big life event we celebrate? Why not divorce announcements? I mean, think about it. You get a nice card, formal language...

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Mr. and Mrs. Steinberg request the honor of your presence at the dissolution of the marriage of their son George Louis Steinberg to Miss Pamela Springfield. And together with the parents of the bride, request that you return all wedding gifts.

jokes.text: They're arranged in order of price. The most expensive are in the front.
jokes.setup: George and Susan browse wedding invitations at a stationery store
jokes.punchline: The salesperson casually admits the most expensive options are placed first — a naked upsell presented as helpful organization
jokes.explanation: A modest observational gag about retail manipulation. The deadpan delivery of an obvious upsell tactic is mildly funny. Functional but not especially crafted.

dialogue.speaker: Susan
dialogue.line: George, these invitations are absolutely hideous. The font is terrible, the colors clash, and the paper quality is awful.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: So what? They're invitations. You send them out, people come. What difference does it make what they look like?

dialogue.speaker: Susan
dialogue.line: What difference does it make? George, people will judge us based on these invitations!

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Judge us? They're getting a free meal. They should be grateful we're inviting them at all. I don't care if the invitation is written on a napkin.

jokes.text: George points to the cheapest, ugliest invitations. Susan: 'Oh, no. George, that's so ugly. We don't want that.' George: 'What's the difference? You just read it and mail it right back.'
jokes.setup: George gravitates to the cheapest, discontinued invitations
jokes.punchline: His complete indifference — 'you just read it and mail it right back' — reveals he views the wedding as something to be minimally survived
jokes.explanation: The 'you just read it and mail it right back' line is perfectly George — complete lack of romance or investment, treating a wedding invitation like junk mail. Strong character integration. Gains rewatch value knowing the full arc of this episode.

dialogue.speaker: Store Clerk
dialogue.line: The glue on these invitations isn't very good.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: So just buy some glue separately and use that.

jokes.text: Why don't they make them anymore? 'Well, for one thing, the glue isn't very adhesive. It takes a lot of moisture to make them stick.' 'So we pick up some Elmer's.'
jokes.setup: The stationer explains the discontinued invitations have toxic/weak glue
jokes.punchline: George's instant solution — just use Elmer's — is both cheap and unknowingly ominous given what the toxic glue will later do
jokes.explanation: This is the setup for the entire episode's dark payoff. On first watch it's a decent George-cheapness gag. On rewatch, the information that the glue requires 'a lot of moisture' and that they use the cheap ones anyway is the seeds of Susan's death. Exceptional rewatch value. Character integration is strong — George's cheapness literally causes the tragedy.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Hey Lily!

dialogue.speaker: Susan
dialogue.line: It's Susan.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Yeah, I know. But you look like a Lily.

jokes.text: George to Susan, gesturing at the cheap invitations: 'Do you see what I do for you?'
jokes.setup: George has just argued for the cheapest, ugliest, discontinued invitations
jokes.punchline: He frames settling for the absolute minimum as a grand sacrifice on his part
jokes.explanation: A perfect single-line character joke. George's self-aggrandizing martyrdom at choosing the worst possible option as if it's a gift is quintessential Costanza. Economy is near-perfect — the whole joke is delivered in one short phrase.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Jerry, I can't do this. I can't get married. This is insane!

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I don't even like myself and you want me to spend the rest of my life with someone else?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: What am I thinking? I'm going to end up miserable, broke, and bald!

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Well, I'm already bald. But the other stuff!

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Jerry, you gotta help me. You gotta talk me out of this. Please!

jokes.text: Kramer calls Susan 'Lilly.' She corrects him. He insists: 'Well, you look like a Lilly.'
jokes.setup: Kramer greets George's fiancée by the wrong name
jokes.punchline: When corrected, rather than apologizing, he doubles down — 'you look like a Lilly' — as if his perception overrides her actual name
jokes.explanation: Kramer refusing to accept correction because he's committed to his own reality is perfectly in character. The confidence of 'well, you look like a Lilly' is the key — it's not an apology, it's a competing claim. This joke pays off later when he says 'Poor Lilly' at Susan's death, making it a running gag with dark resonance.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You should break up with Susan.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I can't break up with her. That would be so awkward.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: It's one conversation, George.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I'd rather spend the rest of my life miserable with her than have to sit through that talk.

jokes.text: George spiraling about the wedding: 'It's here. It's happening. Can I do this? I can't do this. Look at me. Look at me, I can't do this.'
jokes.setup: George tells Jerry the invitations were ordered, making the wedding feel real and unstoppable
jokes.punchline: His existential panic cascade — completely over-the-top but emotionally honest for the character
jokes.explanation: The comedy here is entirely in Jason Alexander's performance and the absurdity of George treating the wedding like a terminal diagnosis. Strong character work but not particularly crafted on the page.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I'm going to write her a letter. A very detailed letter, explaining everything.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: And then I'm going to flee the country. I'm thinking China. You know, hide among the population.

jokes.text: George: 'What about a letter?' 'A letter?' 'I write a letter and then I go to China. I disappear in a sea of people for, like, six months, a year.'
jokes.setup: Jerry suggests George break up with Susan; George says he can't face the scene
jokes.punchline: George's breakup plan involves writing a vague letter and literally fleeing to China to hide among the population
jokes.explanation: The China plan is delightfully George — a maximally impractical solution to a problem of basic adult communication. 'Disappear in a sea of people' is a great phrase. The escalating absurdity feels earned because it tracks with his character.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: What are you talking about? Staten Island is not China.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: It might as well be.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: What do you mean? I can get to Staten Island in twenty minutes.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Exactly. So you hide there for twenty minutes, and then you come back.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: That's not hiding, that's... that's just going to Staten Island.

jokes.text: George's draft letter: 'Dear Susan, I'm sorry. I made a terrible mistake. I'm really, really sorry.' Jerry: 'That's it?' George: 'What, too short?'
jokes.setup: George proposes writing a breakup letter to Susan instead of confronting her
jokes.punchline: The entire letter is three sentences of apology with no explanation — and George genuinely doesn't understand why it's inadequate
jokes.explanation: The gap between how bad the letter is and George's confidence in it is the core of the joke. 'What, too short?' — as if adding one more line would fix it — is a perfect George line. Strong structure: the letter itself is the setup, 'too short?' is the punchline, and Jerry's silence is the button.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: What if I leave something behind? What if I forget a shirt or something?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Then you leave it behind. You know what that is? That's freedom. That's what freedom is. Freedom is leaving your shirt in a hotel room.

jokes.text: Jerry: 'So write a letter and move to Staten Island. It's easier to blend into a sea of people in Staten Island than China.'
jokes.setup: George can't go to China because of his job; they need a revised plan
jokes.punchline: Jerry pivots to Staten Island as the budget version of disappearing — with deadpan logic that treats Staten Island as equivalent to a billion-person nation
jokes.explanation: The joke works on two levels: the absurdity of comparing Staten Island to China, and the NYC-specific dig at Staten Island as an uninviting place where one might lose oneself. Clean, economical, smart.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I wish Susan would take a plane somewhere.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Why? You want something to happen to her?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: No, no... I just think it would be nice if she went away for a while.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: George, planes crash. The odds are a million to one.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Yeah? Well, I like those odds.

jokes.text: George: 'How do I get the rest of my clothes?' Jerry: 'You come back for your clothes.' George: 'I'm not going back in there.' Jerry: 'So forget about your clothes.' George: 'Well, I'm not starting up a whole new wardrobe now!'
jokes.setup: George is planning to flee to Staten Island to avoid breaking up with Susan
jokes.punchline: His biggest practical objection to abandoning his life is having to replace his wardrobe — prioritizing clothes over freedom from an unwanted marriage
jokes.explanation: Perfect George priorities — the wardrobe concern is funnier and more urgent to him than an unwanted marriage. The escalation across several lines is well-structured, building to the absurd conclusion.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: I'm not an usher, I'm not a bridesmaid... what am I, chopped liver?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: You're a guest.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: A guest? I've known you for years! I should at least be a usher!

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I'll talk to Susan about it.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: You better. Because if I'm not in that wedding party, I'm not coming to the wedding.

jokes.text: Jerry: 'Freedom and no clothes is a lot better than no freedom with clothes.'
jokes.setup: George refuses to give up his wardrobe as the price of escape
jokes.punchline: Jerry offers this chiastic philosophical statement about freedom vs. clothing as if it's a profound aphorism
jokes.explanation: This is one of the most quotable lines in the episode. The chiastic structure ('freedom and no clothes' / 'no freedom with clothes') gives it an aphoristic quality. It's absurd life advice stated with total confidence. Excellent economy and structure.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: How do I break up with her?

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Just take up smoking.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: What?

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: She hates smoking. If you start smoking, she'll break up with you.

jokes.text: George: 'If she would just take a plane somewhere.' Jerry: 'And what, hope for a crash?' George: 'It happens.' Jerry: 'Do you know the odds on a crash? It's a million to one.' George: 'It's something. It's hope.'
jokes.setup: George fantasizes about Susan disappearing so he doesn't have to break up with her
jokes.punchline: George is literally hoping for a plane crash as his best-case scenario — and defends it as 'hope'
jokes.explanation: This is the darkest and funniest joke in the episode. George hoping for a plane crash is shocking but perfectly in character — he's too cowardly to break up and too selfish to care about anyone else. The exchange is perfectly structured: Jerry's incredulity, George's math, and then the devastating reframe of 'It's something. It's hope.' That final line is exceptional — it treats mass tragedy as a reasonable contingency plan. Comparable in darkness and craft to the show's best moments. The rewatch irony (Susan dies anyway, from George's cheap invitations) makes this even richer.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Everyone's getting married. George is getting married, Susan's picking out china patterns...

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: So what? You'll find someone.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Yeah, but what if I don't? What if I end up alone... with just Kramer?

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Hey!

jokes.text: Elaine's primary concern about the wedding: 'What am I gonna get to be in the wedding party?'
jokes.setup: George is in existential crisis about the wedding
jokes.punchline: Elaine's sole concern is her own title/status in the wedding party — completely ignoring George's feelings
jokes.explanation: Classic Elaine self-absorption. Functionally funny because it cuts against any emotional sincerity of the scene. Not the sharpest version of this joke type but well-delivered in context.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: It's a periscope for your car. You mount it on the roof, and you can see over the cars in front of you.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Why would you want to see over the cars in front of you?

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: So you know what's coming!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: What do you mean, what's coming? You're in a car. The only thing that's coming is the car in front of you. That's why you're looking at it.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: But with the periscope, you can see further ahead.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: And then what? You're still stuck behind the same car. Now you're just aware of it earlier. That's not helpful, that's just depressing.

jokes.text: Elaine, about getting her wedding role: 'You don't ask. You tell.'
jokes.setup: George says he'll 'ask' Susan about making Elaine an usher
jokes.punchline: Elaine corrects his language with imperious confidence — she's treating this as a management issue, not a social request
jokes.explanation: Four words, maximum attitude. Elaine's confidence and entitlement is perfectly captured. Near-perfect economy. Could be a bit more character-specific to rank higher.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You know what? Stupid! Stupid! Stupid! Stupid!

jokes.text: Elaine's suggestion for George to end the relationship: 'Start smoking.'
jokes.setup: George asks Elaine what a guy could do to end a relationship without a direct confrontation
jokes.punchline: Elaine's immediate, confident answer is 'start smoking' — a completely unexpected left turn
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. The misdirection is superb — after George has been workshopping elaborate escape plans (China, Staten Island), Elaine cuts through with a two-word solution that's both brilliant and insane. The internal logic works: it's annoying enough to drive someone away without a direct confrontation. Perfect economy. It then immediately sets up the George-smoking subplot.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You know, if you're really bothered by this whole near-death experience, you could always just walk into traffic yourself. I mean, if that bothers you.

jokes.text: Elaine suggests George start smoking to drive Susan away. George: 'Smoking.' A pause, then he takes it completely seriously.
jokes.setup: George asks what a guy could do to end a relationship without having 'the scene'
jokes.punchline: Elaine's 'start smoking' as a genuine relationship sabotage strategy — and George's immediate sincere consideration of it
jokes.explanation: The smoking idea is genuinely original as a breakup strategy — it's not a joke you've heard before. George taking it seriously instead of finding it absurd makes it funnier. Sets up a physical running gag that pays off in the next scene.

dialogue.speaker: Jeannie
dialogue.line: A collar shirt is a waste. You wear it, you don't see the collar. It's right here, you can't see it.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You see it when you look down.

dialogue.speaker: Jeannie
dialogue.line: When do you look down? You're not looking down all the time. And when you do look down, you see your pants. You don't see your collar. No one sees your collar. But you got to buy the collar. You got to tuck it in.

jokes.text: Jerry: 'I'm sick of being single. I'm getting out.' George: 'So it's just gonna be me and Kramer?' Jerry: 'Yeah, just you and Kramer.' [pause] George: 'Me and Kramer.'
jokes.setup: Jerry announces he's getting out of the single life; George is already anxious about losing friends to marriage
jokes.punchline: George's dawning horror as he processes life with only Kramer as his companion — the pause and repetition sell it
jokes.explanation: The joke is in the reaction — George's quiet 'Me and Kramer' lands because the audience understands exactly what that fate entails. Good structural beat with Kramer entering immediately after.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Hi, I'm Jerry.

dialogue.speaker: Jeannie
dialogue.line: Oh, hi! I'm Jeannie.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Wait, J.J.? Me too!

dialogue.speaker: Jeannie
dialogue.line: No way! That's amazing!

jokes.text: Kramer immediately appears after George mutters 'Me and Kramer' and announces: 'Hey, I thought of a great invention for driving. A periscope in a car, so you can see traffic.'
jokes.setup: George just despaired about being left with Kramer as his only companion
jokes.punchline: Kramer's entrance with an idiotic invention immediately confirms George's fears
jokes.explanation: The timing of Kramer's entrance is a masterclass in sitcom structure. George's despair is immediately justified by Kramer's submarine-car periscope idea. The callback works perfectly because it's not stated — the audience makes the connection themselves.

dialogue.speaker: Susan
dialogue.line: What if Kramer falls during the ceremony?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: He won't fall.

dialogue.speaker: Susan
dialogue.line: But what if he does? What if he trips on the aisle and goes sprawling in front of everyone?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Susan, he's an usher, not a trapeze artist.

jokes.text: Jerry systematically dismantling the periscope car idea: 'How are you gonna drive while you're looking through a periscope? Besides which, it's not a submarine. There's no room for a periscope on a car.' Kramer: 'Oh, you make a higher roof.' Jerry: 'They're not making higher roofs.'
jokes.setup: Kramer proposes a periscope in a car
jokes.punchline: Jerry's methodical takedown of each logical flaw, with Kramer having an absurd counter for each point
jokes.explanation: The pleasure is in Jerry's straight-man frustration and Kramer's complete conviction. 'You make a higher roof' escalates the absurdity nicely. Solid but not the sharpest Kramer-idea bit the show has done.

dialogue.speaker: Susan
dialogue.line: George, are you smoking?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: What? No, no. I'm a big smoker. I smoke all the time.

dialogue.speaker: Susan
dialogue.line: You just told me you quit smoking years ago.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I did quit. But then I started again. I'm a very heavy smoker now.

jokes.text: After saving a woman from being hit by a car, Kramer says: 'Shouldn't there be some kind of reward for that?' and 'You should be careful crossing the street. Otherwise you could die, if that bothers you.'
jokes.setup: Kramer heroically saves a woman from being hit by a car
jokes.punchline: He immediately demands a reward, then lectures the woman with the caveat 'if that bothers you' — as if death might not be a concern for everyone
jokes.explanation: 'If that bothers you' is a wonderfully absurd qualifier. Kramer's simultaneous heroism and mercenary expectation of reward, plus the patronizing death-warning with an opt-out clause, is perfectly in character. Two distinct jokes in a short exchange.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I'll have the cereal. With the milk on the side.

dialogue.speaker: Waitress
dialogue.line: The milk on the side?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I like to control the milk-to-cereal ratio.

dialogue.speaker: Jeannie
dialogue.line: I'll have the same thing.

jokes.text: Kramer, immediately after the near-death experience: 'What? The shirt under your sweater. It sits in a drawer for three weeks and when it finally comes out, it only sticks up out of your collar.'
jokes.setup: A woman was just nearly killed and Jeannie is recovering; Kramer is expected to say something meaningful
jokes.punchline: Instead he pivots to a genuine pet peeve about undershirt visibility — a complete tonal non-sequitur
jokes.explanation: The tonal whiplash is the joke — life-or-death moment immediately followed by a sincere complaint about fashion. Very Kramer. The observation itself (undershirts only appearing at the collar) has real observational merit.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I'm in love with myself. I've actually swept myself off my feet.

jokes.text: Jerry meets Jeannie: 'Same initials. How do you like that?' Jeannie: 'I like it.'
jokes.setup: Jerry meets Jeannie Steinman after she's nearly hit by a car
jokes.punchline: Jerry's opening romantic gambit is their shared initials — a very minor coincidence elevated to a meaningful connection; she matches his energy
jokes.explanation: Sets up the Jeannie-as-Jerry's-mirror subplot. The matching enthusiasm about shared initials tells us immediately she's his mirror — they're both impressed by nothing. Functional scene-setter.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: What are you, insane? You can't date yourself!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Why not? I'm perfect for me. We have everything in common.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: That's the most selfish thing I've ever heard. What happens when you get mad at yourself? You just break up with yourself?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Well, we'd work it out.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: This is unbelievable. You're gonna end up alone, talking to yourself at Monk's.

jokes.text: Susan catches George smoking. George: 'I've always smoked.' Susan: 'I've never seen you smoke.' George: 'Oh, yeah, well, big smoker.'
jokes.setup: George is trying the smoking-as-repellent strategy; Susan walks in
jokes.punchline: George's transparent lie — 'I've always smoked' — and the ridiculous self-description 'big smoker'
jokes.explanation: 'Big smoker' is a great George line — the improvised confidence of a bad liar. The callback to Elaine's advice paying off here lands well. George's commitment to a completely unbelievable lie is character-perfect.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: I want $100 for not being greeted with 'hello'

dialogue.speaker: Bank Teller
dialogue.line: But I said 'hey'

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: 'Hey' is not 'hello'

dialogue.speaker: Bank Teller
dialogue.line: 'Hey' and 'hello' are the same thing

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: No they're not! 'Hello' is a formal greeting. 'Hey' is what you say to your friends

jokes.text: Jerry and Jeannie both ordering cereal at a restaurant. Waiter: 'The usual?' Jerry: 'Yeah.' Jeannie: 'A bowl of Cheerios, not too much milk.' Waiter: 'Okay, two bowls of Cheerios.' Jerry: 'You too?'
jokes.setup: Jerry and Jeannie are on a date at a restaurant
jokes.punchline: They both independently order the same unusual cereal order — reinforcing that Jeannie is Jerry's mirror
jokes.explanation: Cereal in a restaurant is already a Jerry quirk; Jeannie sharing it exactly confirms they're the same person. The 'not too much milk' specificity is a nice detail. Functional character-mirror joke.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I'm at the singles table. I'm surrounded by losers.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: George, you ARE a loser.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I know, but I don't like to be around other losers. It makes me feel like I'm part of a group.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: What's wrong with that?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I don't want to be part of a group of losers. I want to be a lone loser.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: How is that better?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Because then I have the freedom to meet a woman. I need to be unfettered, unencumbered by the stigma of the singles table.

jokes.text: Jerry: 'Hey, did you hear the bank on the corner is offering $100 if you go in there and they don't greet you with a 'hello.''
jokes.setup: Jerry introduces the bank's promotional policy
jokes.punchline: The premise itself — a bank paying $100 for not saying 'hello' — is an absurd corporate promotion that will fuel an entire subplot
jokes.explanation: This is more setup than joke, but the absurdity of the premise (a bank's entire policy hinging on the word 'hello' vs. 'hey') is the comedic engine for several scenes. Scored primarily as scene-setting.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You know what? I'm getting a prenup just so I have an exit strategy. Like, if things go south, I'm out.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: That's the most depressing thing I've ever heard.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: What? It's practical. It's like having a parachute before you jump out of the plane.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: No, it's like jumping out of the plane before it even takes off.

jokes.text: Jerry: 'She's just like me. She talks like me. She acts like me. She ordered cereal in a restaurant. We even have the same initials.' George: 'Wait, I just realized what's going on. Now I know what I've been looking for all these years. Myself. I've been waiting for me to come along. And now I've swept myself off my feet.'
jokes.setup: Jerry lists all the ways Jeannie is exactly like him
jokes.punchline: George's synthesis: Jerry has fallen in love with himself. 'I've swept myself off my feet.'
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best lines. 'I've swept myself off my feet' is a perfectly constructed paradox — the action and recipient of a romantic idiom are the same person. It's quotable, well-structured, and gets at a real Seinfeld theme (Jerry's narcissism). The buildup through listing the shared traits makes the payoff feel earned.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I think we should sign a prenup.

dialogue.speaker: Susan
dialogue.line: A prenup? George, what assets are you protecting? Your Tupperware collection?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I have... things of value.

dialogue.speaker: Susan
dialogue.line: Oh absolutely, I'm trembling at the thought of getting half of your nothing. Where do I sign?

jokes.text: George's reaction to Jerry's self-love revelation: 'Will you stop it, man? You're freaking me out. Jeez.'
jokes.setup: Jerry is enchanted by his own reflection in Jeannie; George is supposed to be processing this
jokes.punchline: George's visceral discomfort — 'you're freaking me out' — as if Jerry's narcissism has crossed into the uncanny valley
jokes.explanation: Solid reaction beat. George as the voice of (relative) normalcy works, and his disgust at Jerry's solipsism is relatable. Not especially crafted but earns its laugh.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: So what's the difference between 'hey' and 'hello'?

dialogue.speaker: Jeannie
dialogue.line: Well, 'hey' is more casual, 'hello' is more formal.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Yeah, yeah, that makes sense.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Oh wow, what a big surprise. You two actually agree on something.

jokes.text: Elaine at the bank demands $100 because the teller said 'hey' instead of 'hello.' Teller: 'Well, 'hey' is 'hello.' Same thing.' Elaine: 'The ad said the bank will pay $100 if you're not greeted with a 'hello.' You're taking that much too literally.'
jokes.setup: Elaine tries to collect the $100 bank greeting guarantee
jokes.punchline: The bank employee tells Elaine she's taking the literal wording of the bank's own advertisement 'too literally'
jokes.explanation: The institutional hypocrisy of a bank accusing a customer of taking the bank's explicit promotional language 'too literally' is sharp and incisive. It's a classic Seinfeld bit about bureaucratic absurdity where the institution creates a rule it then refuses to honor. The irony is well-constructed and the frustration is completely relatable.

dialogue.speaker: Susan
dialogue.line: These invitations are cheap! The paper is thin, the printing is barely visible.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: So what? It's just an invitation.

dialogue.speaker: Susan
dialogue.line: Just an invitation? George, this sets the tone for the entire wedding!

dialogue.speaker: Susan
dialogue.line: But I'm insisting on the most expensive rings. We need real platinum, real diamonds.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Wait, wait, wait. You won't spend a nickel on the invitations, but the rings have to be platinum?

dialogue.speaker: Susan
dialogue.line: That's different. The rings are forever.

jokes.text: Elaine finds out Kramer is no longer an usher: 'What are you talking about?' George: 'You've been demoted.' Kramer: 'Why?' George: 'Because you called her by the wrong name.' Kramer: 'But she really looks like a Lilly.'
jokes.setup: Kramer is told he's been demoted from usher because he called Susan 'Lilly'
jokes.punchline: Kramer's defense: 'But she really looks like a Lilly' — still committed to his original perception
jokes.explanation: The running gag of Kramer's insistence on 'Lilly' is developed here. His unflinching defense — still insisting she looks like a Lilly — demonstrates his total commitment to his own worldview. Gets rewatch value from the final 'Poor Lilly' callback.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: What's wrong with me? What's wrong with ME? I'll tell you what's wrong with me, Jerry. You're dating your clone. Your female clone. Do you understand what that means? You're essentially dating yourself. That's not a relationship, that's narcissism with a vagina.

jokes.text: Elaine learns she's not an usher. 'So I'm nothing? Jerry is best man, Kramer is an usher, and I am nothing?'
jokes.setup: Elaine's wedding-role grievance from earlier
jokes.punchline: The word 'nothing' landing as the existential summary of her status — elevated by the comparison structure
jokes.explanation: The list structure ('Jerry is X, Kramer is Y, I am nothing') is a classic comedy construction. 'Nothing' is the right word — plays on the show's own 'show about nothing' identity. Competent but expected.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So what you're saying is, you don't like her because she's like me?

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: I'm saying I can't be friends with someone who's exactly like you. It's too much.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Well then I guess we can't be friends anymore either.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: What? No! Jerry, you can't—we've been friends for years!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You just said you can't be friends with someone like me.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: That's different! You're... you're YOU!

jokes.text: George: 'Oh, this is great! Now I'm gonna be stuck at the singles' table with all the losers?' Kramer: 'Weddings are a great place to meet chicks. I have to be unfettered.'
jokes.setup: Jerry announces he's bringing a date to the wedding; George is now without a date
jokes.punchline: At his own wedding, George is worried about being at the singles' table; Kramer explains he needs to be 'unfettered' to meet women
jokes.explanation: Two good character moments: George's inability to view his own wedding from anything but his own social anxiety, and Kramer's word choice 'unfettered' — an oddly formal word for picking up women at a wedding.

dialogue.speaker: Bank Manager
dialogue.line: Alright, alright, let's settle down. We need to take this seriously. I'm calling an emergency committee meeting to discuss the 'hey' versus 'hello' situation.

dialogue.speaker: Bank Manager
dialogue.line: This is a matter of protocol. We can't have our customers confused about proper greeting procedures.

dialogue.speaker: Bank Manager
dialogue.line: I'm drafting a memo. 'Effective immediately, all tellers will use 'hello.' 'Hey' is no longer acceptable in this establishment.'

jokes.text: Elaine's escape plan advice: 'All right. I got two words for you: Prenup.' George: 'Prenup, what does that mean?' Jerry: 'Ask her to sign a prenup.' George: 'What does that do?' Jerry: 'Because most women, when asked to sign a prenup, are so offended, they back out of the marriage.' George: 'They are?' Elaine: 'I wouldn't sign one.'
jokes.setup: The group brainstorms ways for George to escape the wedding
jokes.punchline: Elaine's prenup suggestion followed by her immediate personal declaration that she wouldn't sign one — unknowingly validating the strategy
jokes.explanation: Elaine accidentally proving her own strategy's validity by refusing a hypothetical prenup is a clever structural joke. The exchange flows naturally and the group-brainstorm energy is fun. The comedy builds through George's genuinely not knowing what a prenup is.

dialogue.speaker: Bank Manager
dialogue.line: Alright, we've decided on a compromise. Instead of the full $100 greeting, we'll offer a $20 H-greeting.

jokes.text: George presents the prenup to Susan. She laughs. George: 'What's so funny?' Susan: 'You don't have any money. I make more money than you do.' George's silent reaction.
jokes.setup: George nervously introduces the prenuptial agreement hoping Susan will be offended and back out
jokes.punchline: Susan's laughter reveals George has no assets to protect — she literally makes more money — destroying his plan and his dignity simultaneously
jokes.explanation: This is one of the sharpest jokes in the episode. The reversal is perfectly constructed: George's escape plan is predicated on Susan being offended, but it backfires completely because George has nothing worth protecting. The added humiliation that she earns more is perfectly in character with the George-as-loser dynamic. Susan's laughter is the punchline and it lands devastatingly. 'Yeah, give me the papers, I'll sign them' is a brilliant button — she's signing with amusement, not offense.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I'm engaged! We're in love, we're getting married, it's going to be beautiful. There's going to be flowers, there's going to be music, candles...

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You know what? I don't care about all that crap.

jokes.text: Susan, signing the prenup while laughing: 'Yeah, give me the papers, I'll sign them.'
jokes.setup: George's prenup plan was supposed to offend Susan into backing out
jokes.punchline: She gleefully signs it, completing the humiliation of George's escape plan
jokes.explanation: The button on the prenup joke. Susan's cheerful compliance is the perfect final humiliation. The economy of the line — delivered while laughing — makes it land harder than any elaborate retort would.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So what's the deal with coffee? I mean, we're all just standing in line, paying money for hot bean water. And we act like it's sophisticated. 'Oh, I'll have a macchiato.' It's a fancy word for 'I'm pretending this matters.'

dialogue.speaker: Jeannie
dialogue.line: I just want coffee.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Exactly! You want coffee. But instead you gotta decide between seventeen options, each one named after a European city you'll never visit. And the sizes? Small, medium, large — no, wait — tall, grande, venti. Who decided this? What was wrong with small?

jokes.text: Jeannie to Jerry: 'Well, I went in there and they said, 'hey.'' Jerry: 'Yeah, I think it's the same thing.' Elaine's audible 'Oh, big surprise.' reaction.
jokes.setup: Jeannie also tried to collect the $100 bank greeting guarantee and got the same 'hey' response
jokes.punchline: Jerry immediately validates Jeannie's identical conclusion; Elaine's disgusted 'Oh, big surprise' — she sees exactly what Jerry-plus-Jerry looks like
jokes.explanation: Two callbacks converging: the bank 'hey' vs. 'hello' debate, and Jeannie being Jerry's mirror. Elaine's reaction is the best part — her exasperation at two Jerrys agreeing about everything is relatable and funny.

dialogue.speaker: Jeannie
dialogue.line: So if brunch is breakfast and lunch combined, why stop there? We should have 'lupper' - that's lunch and dinner. And 'linner' - late lunch into dinner.

jokes.text: George's reaction on seeing the cheap invitations: 'Oh, these are so cheap.'
jokes.setup: The invitations George cheaply chose have arrived
jokes.punchline: George complains about their cheapness — as if this is a surprise to anyone, including himself
jokes.explanation: George's self-unawareness about the cheapness he himself insisted on is mildly funny. More of a story beat than a crafted joke, but the irony lands. Gains rewatch value as foreshadowing of the toxic glue.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Wait a minute. If I'm dating my clone, that means I'm dating someone exactly like me.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: And I hate me. I really do. I can't stand being around myself.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I need my opposite. I need someone completely different from me.

jokes.text: Elaine dislikes Jeannie: 'Everything she thinks, you think. Everything you think, she thinks. No, I can't take it. I can't take it, Jerry. It's too much. It's too much.' Jerry: 'If you can't take her, maybe you can't take me.' Elaine: 'So that's how it's gonna be?' Jerry: 'That's how it's gonna be.' Both: 'Oh, God help us!'
jokes.setup: Elaine confesses she hates Jeannie
jokes.punchline: Jerry points out that if Elaine hates Jeannie (who is exactly like Jerry), she must hate Jerry — they both realize this and are horrified simultaneously
jokes.explanation: Well-constructed logical trap that both characters walk into simultaneously. 'So that's how it's gonna be?' / 'That's how it's gonna be' followed by shared 'Oh, God help us!' is a great comedic mirror moment — ironically, they're perfectly in sync even when discussing the problem of being too in sync.

dialogue.speaker: Doctor
dialogue.line: I'm sorry. She's dead.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Dead? You're sure? I mean, you checked?

dialogue.speaker: Doctor
dialogue.line: Yes, I'm sure.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: You did a pretty thorough check?

dialogue.speaker: Doctor
dialogue.line: Yes.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: 'Cause I'd hate to get down there to the funeral and she's not quite ready to go.

jokes.text: Jerry: 'If you can't take her, maybe you can't take me.' Elaine: 'So that's how it's gonna be?' Jerry: 'That's how it's gonna be.' Elaine: 'Oh, God help us!'
jokes.setup: Elaine says she can't stand Jeannie because she's identical to Jerry
jokes.punchline: Jerry's ultimatum that disliking Jeannie = disliking him, and Elaine's horror at the implication
jokes.explanation: 'Oh, God help us!' is a great button. The logic is airtight — if Jeannie is Jerry and Elaine doesn't like Jeannie, then Elaine doesn't like Jerry. Elaine's horror at being caught in this syllogism is funny.

dialogue.speaker: Doctor
dialogue.line: The cause of death was poisoning from the cheap envelopes.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Well, I always said cheap invitations would be the death of me. I just didn't think it would be literal.

jokes.text: The extended bank arbitration scene: manager, Jim, Barbara, Jane, Mike are all summoned to rule on whether 'hey' constitutes 'hello.' Multiple people giving overlapping greetings: 'How you doing? / What's happening? / What's up?'
jokes.setup: Elaine demands arbitration from the bank manager over whether 'hey' is 'hello'
jokes.punchline: The bank convenes a committee of multiple employees to debate the semantics of a greeting, with each employee demonstrating an informal greeting of their own
jokes.explanation: The visual comedy of a bank committee deliberating over greeting semantics is inherently absurd and escalates nicely. Each employee demonstrating an informal greeting — thus making the bank's point moot — is clever. The bureaucratic machinery responding to a trivial matter is very Seinfeld.

dialogue.speaker: Doctor
dialogue.line: The glue on those toxic invitations killed her.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: So I'm gonna get a refund on those invitations. We had like 200 of them printed up.

jokes.text: During the bank deliberation wait, Elaine examines the furniture: 'What is this, oak?' Bank Employee: 'I think it's pine.' Elaine: 'Pine is good.' Employee: 'Yeah, pine's okay.'
jokes.setup: Elaine is left waiting alone while the bank staff deliberates
jokes.punchline: She fills dead time with a completely inconsequential wood identification conversation
jokes.explanation: A perfectly Seinfeldian exchange about nothing. The profundity-free conversation about wood types during a profound bureaucratic standoff is very on-brand. 'Pine is good.' / 'Yeah, pine's okay.' has a nice deadpan rhythm.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Yeah, I heard about Susan. That's terrible. A real tragedy.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: You know, I was just thinking about her the other day. Poor Susanna.

jokes.text: Bank manager's settlement: 'You got a greeting. It starts with an H. How's 20 bucks sound?' Elaine: 'I'll take it.'
jokes.setup: The bank committee has deliberated; the manager returns with a verdict
jokes.punchline: The committee's official ruling is to split the difference — she gets $20 because 'it starts with an H' — and Elaine, who has been fighting righteously for $100, immediately accepts
jokes.explanation: 'It starts with an H' is a brilliantly absurd legal reasoning. The immediate capitulation from Elaine — who was demanding the full amount — is the button. A satisfying resolution to the bank subplot with good economy.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You were supposed to be married by now!

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I know, I know.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: What happened to Susan?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Well, she's... she's gone.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Gone? What do you mean gone?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: She died.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Oh my God!

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Yeah, it was terrible. But anyway, you want some coffee?

jokes.text: Kramer proposes to Jeannie: 'Will you marry me?'
jokes.setup: Kramer has been with Jeannie (Jerry's date/girlfriend) for an unspecified amount of time; no romantic setup has been shown
jokes.punchline: Kramer proposes to a woman Jerry just met — completely out of nowhere, with no setup in the scene
jokes.explanation: The unexplained Kramer proposal is pure absurdist Seinfeld — the show doesn't feel obligated to explain how Kramer got there. It's funny precisely because the context is missing. Serves primarily as setup for the pact payoff.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Hi Marisa, it's George Costanza. Yeah, listen, Susan and I... we're not together anymore. She passed away. But anyway, I was wondering if you'd like to go out sometime?

jokes.text: Kramer to George: 'George! George Costanza, come in here! Georgie boy. George, big news. I'm getting married.' George: 'Married? What?' Kramer: 'September 21st, first day of autumn. Leaves changing, beautiful colours. All that crap.'
jokes.setup: Kramer excitedly announces his engagement
jokes.punchline: 'All that crap' — Kramer romanticizes an autumn wedding and then immediately dismisses the romance as 'crap'
jokes.explanation: 'All that crap' is a perfect Kramer tag — he sells the romance of autumn, then immediately undercuts it with total self-awareness. The whiplash between genuine enthusiasm and dismissal is very in character.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Yeah, so my fiancée Susan just died.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: But I'm still available Saturday night if you want to grab dinner.

jokes.text: Kramer: 'You see? I kept up my end of the pact.'
jokes.setup: Kramer reveals there was a pact between him and George about getting married
jokes.punchline: Kramer has somehow fulfilled a marriage pact — by proposing to a woman Jerry just met — while George remains trapped in a wedding he's desperate to escape
jokes.explanation: The pact reveal (referenced earlier in the season, Part 1) is a nice structural payoff. Kramer fulfilling 'his end' by proposing to someone he just met, while George is paralyzed with regret, is a great ironic juxtaposition.

jokes.text: Jerry: 'This has been quite a night. I could sure use a cup of coffee.' Jeannie: 'Hey, what's the deal with decaf? How do they get the caffeine out of there? And then where does it go?'
jokes.setup: Jerry and Jeannie are celebrating with Kramer after the proposal
jokes.punchline: Jeannie launches into a 'what's the deal with...' stand-up-style observation — Jerry's exact comedic voice — complete with the Seinfeld cadence
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's most meta joke. Jeannie doing Jerry's exact 'what's the deal with...' routine — the show's signature observational comedy style — and getting it right is brilliantly self-aware. It works as character comedy (Jeannie IS Jerry), as meta-comedy (the show mocking its own format), and as a setup for Jerry's horror at hearing himself from outside. One of the episode's standout moments.

jokes.text: Jeannie: 'More cereal? That's your third bowl today. You had it for breakfast and lunch.' Jerry: 'Hey. So, what's the deal with brunch? If it's a combination of breakfast and lunch, how come there's no 'lupper,' or no 'linner'?'
jokes.setup: After Jeannie does Jerry's 'what's the deal' bit, Jerry responds with his own — about brunch
jokes.punchline: 'Lupper' and 'linner' — Jerry proposes neologisms for missing meal combinations with complete Seinfeld-show seriousness
jokes.explanation: 'Lupper' and 'linner' are classic Seinfeld neologisms. The brunch bit is meta: it's the show doing its own material through two characters who are both the show's star. The word-coinage is the kind of thing Seinfeld is famous for, and it works here as both a joke and as character commentary. 'Linner' in particular has a nice sound.

jokes.text: Jerry: 'So, what's the deal with brunch? If it's a combination of breakfast and lunch, how come there's no "lupper" or no "linner"?'
jokes.setup: Jeannie ordering cereal prompts a food-meal timing observation
jokes.punchline: 'Lupper' and 'linner' as the logical extensions of 'brunch' logic — the neologisms are the joke
jokes.explanation: 'Lupper' and 'linner' are genuine Seinfeld neologisms — the exact kind of wordplay the show is known for. The observation is solid and the invented words are both logical and funny. High quotability.

jokes.text: Jerry: 'I think I may have made a big mistake. I realized what the problem is. I can't be with someone like me. I hate myself!'
jokes.setup: Jerry has been in love with his mirror image; Kramer pushed him to commit
jokes.punchline: Jerry's epiphany is not that Jeannie isn't right for him — it's that he HATES himself, therefore he can't be with her
jokes.explanation: 'I hate myself' as the logical conclusion of narcissistic self-examination is a great joke. It's both a character revelation and a comedic inversion — falling in love with yourself only works if you actually like yourself. The line is delivered in the context of a breakup reason, making it funnier. High quotability.

jokes.text: Kramer: 'It's too bad you got engaged.' Jerry [flatly]: 'Yeah, too bad.'
jokes.setup: Jerry has just said he made a big mistake by getting engaged to Jeannie
jokes.punchline: Kramer offers fake sympathy; Jerry's deflated 'yeah, too bad' accepts it without protest
jokes.explanation: A low-key beat that works mostly in timing and delivery. The gap between the problem and the 'sympathy' is funny but not especially crafted.

jokes.text: George rushes to the hospital. The doctor: 'Excuse me, are you the husband?' George: 'Well, not yet. Fiancé.'
jokes.setup: George arrives at the hospital after Susan collapses
jokes.punchline: In a medical emergency, George pauses to make the technical distinction between 'husband' and 'fiancé' — unable to suppress his pedantry even in crisis
jokes.explanation: The 'not yet' is a great character beat — George's compulsive precision even when his fiancée may be dying. The 'not yet' also carries the dark irony that he didn't want to be the husband at all. Rewatch value is high — this small word has enormous dramatic resonance.

jokes.text: Doctor: 'I'm sorry. She's gone.' George: 'What's that?' Doctor: 'She expired.' George: 'Are you sure?'
jokes.setup: The doctor has come to inform George of Susan's fate
jokes.punchline: George's response to being told his fiancée has died is a bureaucratic 'What's that?' — as if he misheard a bill amount — followed by 'Are you sure?' as if death can be contested
jokes.explanation: 'Are you sure?' in response to a death pronouncement is one of the episode's darkest and best moments. George's inability to process or appropriately respond to tragedy — he sounds like he's questioning a parking ticket — is pitch-perfect character writing. The doctor's patient 'Yes, of course' makes it even funnier.

jokes.text: George to the doctor, after hearing Susan has died: 'Let me ask you... had she been exposed to any kind of inexpensive glue?'
jokes.setup: Susan has just died and George is still processing
jokes.punchline: George's first diagnostic question is about 'inexpensive glue' — he already suspects what happened and is connecting the dots about the cheap invitations he insisted on
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's (and arguably the season's) standout comedic moment. The callback is devastating: George's penny-pinching on invitations has killed his fiancée, and his first question to the doctor reveals he already suspects the connection. 'Inexpensive glue' is perfect — he can't even say 'toxic' because that would implicate the very cheapness he championed. The line is shocking, funny, dark, perfectly in character, and earns every one of its laughs from groundwork laid 18 minutes earlier. It meets the 9+ calibration anchor standard.

jokes.text: Doctor: 'We found traces of a certain toxic adhesive commonly found in very low-priced envelopes.' George: 'Well, she was sending out our wedding invitations. That's probably what did it.'
jokes.setup: The doctor explains the cause of death — toxic glue from cheap envelopes
jokes.punchline: George confirms this as if solving a mundane logistical puzzle — 'that's probably what did it' — with no apparent grief
jokes.explanation: 'That's probably what did it' is devastating in its casualness. George connects the cause of death with the demeanor of someone solving a mild inconvenience. The callback to the stationery scene — where George insisted on the cheap invitations and Susan specifically was the one licking them — pays off completely. The structural irony: George's cheapness killed the person he was trying to escape marrying.

jokes.text: George: 'We were expecting about 200 people.'
jokes.setup: George has just confirmed his cheap envelopes killed his fiancée
jokes.punchline: He offers the wedding guest count as if it's a relevant medical detail, or perhaps as a measure of the tragedy's scale in the only terms he can process
jokes.explanation: Four words that say everything about George Costanza. He cannot access grief; he can only access logistics. The guest count as his response to the death revelation is both devastating and perfectly in character. This line stands alone as a complete character study. It also pays off again when he uses the exact same line on the phone with Marisa Tomei — making it even funnier on rewatch.

jokes.text: George's reaction to Susan's death — 'She's... gone.' — followed by a long pause, then: 'Dead?' and 'I'm so sorry, George.' George: 'Yeah, me too.'
jokes.setup: George delivers the news of Susan's death to Jerry and Elaine
jokes.punchline: George's grief performance is minimal — 'yeah, me too' is his entire emotional response — and the group's reaction to his non-grief is the real comedy
jokes.explanation: George's 'yeah, me too' — a routine social response to condolences — lands as the show's darkest joke because there's no grief underneath it. The pause before 'Dead?' from Jerry shows the audience the characters' shock. 'Yeah, me too' is one of those responses that reveals everything about George's true feelings about the wedding and Susan.

jokes.text: Kramer, on hearing of Susan's death: 'Poor Lilly.'
jokes.setup: The group learns of Susan's death in the hospital corridor
jokes.punchline: Kramer's condolence uses the wrong name — he never stopped believing she was 'Lilly'
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's most perfectly crafted joke. The running gag of Kramer calling Susan 'Lilly' — established twice before this — pays off in the most unexpected and darkest possible context. A condolence delivered with the wrong name at a death scene. It's funny, it's horrible, it's perfectly Kramer, it's perfectly structured, and it's been earned across the entire episode. 'Poor Lilly' as the punchline to a death is about as dark and precise as sitcom comedy gets. This approaches the '9.5 anchor' level in craft. Jerry correcting 'Susan' after is the button.

jokes.text: Elaine asks how it happened. George: 'Apparently the glue in the wedding invitations was... toxic.' [long pause] Elaine: 'Well, that's weird.'
jokes.setup: Elaine asks for the cause of death
jokes.punchline: George delivers 'toxic' with a pregnant pause; Elaine's entire response is 'Well, that's weird' — the understatement of the year
jokes.explanation: 'Well, that's weird' as the response to learning someone died from wedding invitation glue is a masterpiece of understatement. It's the correct logical response — it IS weird — but the casualness of the delivery in the context of a death scene makes it devastating. Character-perfect for Elaine.

jokes.text: George: 'So I guess... you're not getting married.' [to Jerry]. Jerry: 'Yeah.' [pause] Jerry: 'Well, I thought we'd both be getting married.' George: 'Hey, what can I tell you?' Kramer: 'We had a pact!'
jokes.setup: Susan has just died; the group is processing
jokes.punchline: George immediately pivots from the death to his relief at being free; Jerry's failed engagement is treated as a betrayal of a pact; Kramer's outrage at Jerry for not fulfilling 'the pact'
jokes.explanation: 'We had a pact!' after a death scene is a great comedic pivot — the characters' total inability to experience appropriate grief or remorse is the engine of Seinfeld's dark comedy. George's relief masquerading as casual observation ('so you're not getting married') and Kramer's moral outrage about a social pact in the wake of a tragedy are both perfectly in character.

jokes.text: George: 'Let's get some coffee.' [after Susan's death scene]
jokes.setup: The group has just learned of Susan's death and there's an awkward silence
jokes.punchline: George's instinct is to immediately go for coffee — life returning to its comfort rituals with indecent haste
jokes.explanation: The perfect Seinfeld ending beat before the Marisa Tomei call. 'Let's get some coffee' after a death scene is the show's thesis statement: nothing has really changed, life goes on, and these characters are fundamentally incapable of being altered by events. Maximum economy — three words that perfectly encapsulate the show's nihilism and George's emotional bankruptcy.

jokes.text: George calls Marisa Tomei immediately after the hospital: 'I'm the short, funny, quirky bald man you met a little while ago... I'm not engaged anymore. Well, she died. Toxic glue from the wedding invitations... We were expecting about 200 people. Yeah. Anyway, I've got the funeral tomorrow... but my weekend is pretty wide open... and, you know, I was wondering... Hello? Hello?'
jokes.setup: George is calling Marisa Tomei (a previous subplot reference) the night his fiancée died
jokes.punchline: George uses Susan's death as an opener for a date pitch — including 'toxic glue from the wedding invitations... we were expecting about 200 people' as talking points — and Marisa Tomei hangs up on him
jokes.explanation: The episode's definitive ending joke and one of the great Seinfeld moments. George uses Susan's death — the death he caused through cheapness — as a pickup line to call Marisa Tomei. The callback to '200 people' (used identically with the doctor) shows George has already processed Susan's death as anecdote material. 'But my weekend is pretty wide open' is a perfect escalation. Marisa Tomei hanging up on him is the button. This meets the 9+ calibration anchor: it's character-defining, structurally brilliant, pays off multiple plotlines, and is genuinely shocking and funny. The show's entire moral universe is visible in this one phone call.

jokes.text: George: '...I've got the funeral tomorrow, but my weekend is pretty wide open... and, you know, I was wondering—' [Marisa hangs up]
jokes.setup: George is attempting to ask Marisa Tomei on a date the day after his fiancée's death
jokes.punchline: Marisa hangs up — even she has a limit, and 'I've got the funeral tomorrow but my weekend is wide open' is it
jokes.explanation: 'I've got the funeral tomorrow, but my weekend is pretty wide open' is a perfect final George line — cheerful scheduling language applied to the day after his fiancée's death. The hang-up is the button. George's repeat 'Hello? Hello?' into silence is the ideal episode-ending beat. Total character expression, maximum darkness, perfectly funny.

tmdb_overview: While driving a postal truck, Kramer and Newman spot Jerry's stolen car, containing Elaine's JFK golf clubs.
insight: Two-act finale escalates 46 jokes to 89 — Seinfeld's absurdist density peaks.
